LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains only top-level variable definitions and no executable code outside of the `package()` function, which is not executed during the source fetch or PKGBUILD sourcing steps (`pkgver()` is not defined, so the default from `pkgver` variable is used). The `source` arrays point to the official GitHub releases URL (`https://github.com/stripe/stripe-cli/releases/download/...`) with valid `b2sums` checksums provided. There is no obfuscated code, unexpected network requests, or any commands that would execute at parse time. Running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe.
</details>
<summary>Safe: standard PKGBUILD with no top-level execution.</summary>
</security_assessment>

There are no obfuscated commands, unexpected network requests, or suspicious file operations. The source is pinned to a specific version with integrity verification. No security concerns are present.
</details>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/stripe-cli-bin.git to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dr15ubty...
Cloned repository to /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dr15ubty
Analyzing stripe-cli-bin AUR package (working in /tmp/aur-sleuth/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-dr15ubty) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines sources that point directly to official GitHub releases for the `stripe-cli` tool, using HTTPS URLs from `github.com/stripe/stripe-cli`. The `source` arrays for x86_64 and aarch64 are statically defined and do not involve dynamic or obfuscated commands. Checksums are provided via `b2sums` arrays with full b2sum hashes, ensuring integrity verification of the downloaded binaries.

There are no function calls in the global scope, no command substitutions, and no `pkgver()` function override — the version is hardcoded. As such, sourcing the PKGBUILD will not execute unintended code. The `package()` function is irrelevant here, as it is not executed under `--noprepare --nobuild --nodeps`. Fetching the source is safe and follows standard AUR practices: immutable release assets from the project's official repository, pinned to a specific version with verifiable checksums.

The `--printsrcinfo` and `--nobuild` commands will only evaluate the top-level metadata and source URLs, both of which are trustworthy and non-malicious.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR package with safe sources</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard AUR package with safe sources[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...
makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare successful
Running makepkg --printsrcinfo to get source files for agent...
makepkg --printsrcinfo successful
Reviewing required files...
[0/1] Reviewing PKGBUILD...
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches pre-built binaries for both x86_64 and aarch64 architectures directly from the official GitHub releases page of the Stripe CLI project (stripe/stripe-cli). The source URLs are version-pinned and use HTTPS, which ensures authenticity and integrity. Binary checksums are provided via b2sums for both architectures, matching standard Arch packaging practices for binary packages.

The package() function only installs the downloaded `stripe` binary into `$pkgdir/usr/bin/` with appropriate permissions. There are no suspicious operations such as network calls, file modifications outside the package directory, execution of downloaded code during build, or use of obfuscated commands. All dependencies are explicitly empty, which is expected for a statically linked binary.

The maintainer and contributor metadata are present, and the license (Apache-2.0) matches the upstream project. No post-install hooks, systemd services, or file overrides are included. The package adheres to standard AUR binary packaging norms and shows no signs of tampering or malicious injection.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary AUR package with proper checksums
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard binary AUR package with proper checksums
Reviewed all required files.
Deciding which files to review next...
Decided to review 1 additional files: .nvchecker.toml
Reviewing additional files...
Reviewing additional files...
[0/1] Reviewing .nvchecker.toml...
LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `.nvchecker.toml` is a configuration file for `nvchecker`, a tool used to monitor upstream versions of software. The content defines a single source for the `stripe-cli-bin` package, pulling version information from the official GitHub repository `stripe/stripe-cli` with the `use_latest_release = true` option, which instructs nvchecker to track the latest GitHub release tag. This is a standard and legitimate use of nvchecker for version tracking in AUR packages.

There are no network requests, executable commands, file operations, or any form of code injection present. The configuration is static and declarative, containing only metadata for version tracking. The `prefix = "v"` setting is typical for repositories that tag releases like `v1.0.0`. No data exfiltration, obfuscation, or unexpected behavior is possible from this file alone.

This file poses no security risk. It does not execute code or modify the system, and its use aligns with common AUR maintainer practices for automating version updates.
</details>
<summary>
Valid nvchecker config; no executable code or risks
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/1] Reviewing ...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Valid nvchecker config; no executable code or risks
Reviewed all additional files.
Reviewed additional files.

data.reasoning: Both models reviewed the PKGBUILD and .nvchecker.toml, the only files that materially matter for this package. The PKGBUILD is a standard binary repackaging that downloads pre-built Stripe CLI binaries from official GitHub release URLs over HTTPS, verifies them with b2sums checksums, and installs the binary into /usr/bin via the package() function. There is no prepare/build/check function, no .install hook, no top-level code execution, and no systemd services. This is a textbook safe binary AUR package. Both models converged on SAFE with accurate, well-reasoned explanations.
learnings: The 'shallow (2 files reviewed)' trigger is a false alarm for simple binary packages: stripe-cli-bin only contains PKGBUILD, .SRCINFO, and .nvchecker.toml, so reviewing 2 files is complete coverage. The trigger should account for total file count in the repo rather than absolute review count.

Both models correctly identified the absence of an .install hook and the lack of build/prepare functions as positive safety signals — this reasoning is sound and should be reinforced as a standard checklist item for *-bin packages.

For binary packages, the audit could note that integrity ultimately depends on upstream release artifacts plus checksum pinning; verifying that the b2sums correspond to the actual upstream-published hashes is the only deeper validation possible, though it is generally out of scope for static review.
File-selection behavior was appropriate: both models prioritized PKGBUILD first then the only remaining config file, demonstrating correct triage for minimal-package repos.
